descripcion: Selección curada de plugins de Grasshopper útiles para el análisis y diseño urbano: importación GIS, análisis de red, optimización evolutiva, simulación ambiental, simulación física, morfología y exportación web.

items.descripcion: Pulls OpenStreetMap directly into Grasshopper. Roads, buildings, topography. Your site model in 30 seconds without drawing a single line.
Shapefiles, GeoJSON, raster images, topography — all into Grasshopper. The bridge between the GIS analyst who mapped the site and the designer who shapes it.
Walkability on the network. Drops amenities, computes walking catchments, shows which blocks are served and which are stranded. The plugin that answers: can you walk to it.
Evolutionary optimization you can see. A population of designs displayed visually. You pick the ones that work. The algorithm breeds the next generation from your choices. Galapagos with eyes.
Solar radiation, daylight autonomy, wind comfort, thermal analysis — the environmental simulation stack. Every competition panel with a sun diagram used this.
Physics simulation for urban design. Relax a street grid onto terrain. Settle buildings on slopes. Find the natural paths. Not just fabric tensioning, the physics engine for site planning.
Morphs between geometries. Tween a tower into a courtyard block across a site. Grade density from center to edge. The transition tool for parametric masterplans.
Space syntax inside Grasshopper. Integration, choice, depth, betweenness — computed on your street network without leaving the canvas. The analytical layer most urban designers skip.
Exports your Grasshopper model to the web. Stakeholders view and rotate the 3D masterplan in their browser. No Rhino license needed at the presentation.

descripcion: Selección curada de datasets globales para análisis urbano: usos del suelo, redes, población, asentamientos, elevación, índices espaciales, radiancia nocturna e imágenes satelitales.

items.descripcion: Land use classification for 790 European cities at quarter-hectare resolution. Five levels of sealed surface density. 2006 to 2021. The dataset that knows the difference between a dense block and a loose suburb.
Roads, buildings, transit stops, land use, turn restrictions, surface types. Routable. Queryable. The only open global dataset where network analysis and building footprints live in the same schema.
Gridded population at 100 metres. Disaggregated by age and sex. Tells you where children under five live, where elderly over seventy live. 242 countries. Updated annually.
Global Human Settlement Layer. Classifies every square kilometre on earth into the same 7-tier urban-rural hierarchy. 1975 to 2030. The only dataset where Mumbai and Munich are measured the same way.
The Space Shuttle mapped 80 percent of earth in 11 days in February 2000. 30-metre elevation grid. Still the global baseline for slope analysis, watershed delineation, and viewshed computation.
Built from 180,000 radar images. Detects vertical structures, walls, roofs, not spectral signatures. The only global settlement map that works under permanent cloud cover. DLR. 2011.
Hexagonal spatial index. 16 resolution levels from continent down to a single building. Uniform area, uniform neighbours. The geometry that makes urban analytics composable across scales.
Nighttime radiance at 500 metres. Every night since 2012. Economic activity, blackouts, refugee movements — visible from orbit. Atmospherically corrected. Daily or monthly composites.
10-metre multispectral imagery. Every 5 days. Free. Since 2015. ESA. The satellite that made remote sensing operational for urban analysis without commercial licensing.
JAXA. 30-metre digital surface model from optical stereo. Captures buildings and tree canopy, not just bare earth. Complementary to SRTM where vertical structure matters.
